title: Dog Drooping Lip and Eyelid on One Side: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: If your dog has a drooping face, it could be facial nerve paralysis. Learn about urgency levels, causes, and how photos help your vet diagnose.
content_html:
What Does a Drooping Face Mean?

Seeing a drooping lip, eyelid, or ear on one side of your dog's face can be very concerning. This condition is often caused by facial nerve paralysis or Horner's Syndrome. It indicates that the nerves responsible for controlling the muscles of the face are not functioning correctly. This can happen due to inflammation, ear infections, trauma, or sometimes for no clear reason (idiopathic).

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for a drooping face is Medium. While this is typically not a life-threatening emergency, your dog should be examined by a veterinarian within 24 hours. It is important to rule out underlying issues like a deep inner ear infection or systemic disease. If your dog is also stumbling, tilting their head, or acting extremely lethargic, you should seek care sooner.

Why Taking a Photo is Helpful

Capturing a clear photo or short video of your dog's face while they are relaxed at home is an excellent triage tool. Symptoms can sometimes change or be less obvious when a dog is tense at the veterinary clinic. A photo provides your vet with a baseline of the facial symmetry, helping them determine which specific cranial nerves are involved and how quickly the condition is progressing.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause facial drooping?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the facial nerve passes very close to the middle ear, a severe or deep ear infection can cause inflammation that leads to paralysis of the facial muscles.

faqs.question: Will my dog's face ever return to normal?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the underlying cause. If it is caused by an infection or inflammation that is treated early, many dogs recover fully. In idiopathic cases, some permanent drooping may remain, but dogs usually lead a normal life.

faqs.question: Is my dog in pain when their face droops?
faqs.answer: Generally, the paralysis itself is not painful, though the underlying cause (like an ear infection) might be. The main concern is often eye health, as the dog may not be able to blink effectively, leading to dry eye.
